title: Magnetic Behavior of Ferro-, Antiferro-, and Ferrimagnetic Systems in the Griffiths Phase: A Theoretical Study
abstract: In this report, we provide a theoretical framework for the magnetic behavior of the Griffiths phase, which, along with three-dimensional spin-1/2 Ising ferromagnetic systems, can be extended to antiferromagnetic as well as ferrimagnetic systems. We find that the magnetic behavior in the Griffiths phase of three-dimensional antiferromagnetic and ferrimagnetic systems is more unusual than that of conventional ferromagnetic systems. However, this study offers a possible framework for the identification of Griffiths phase behavior in three-dimensional antiferromagnetic and ferrimagnetic systems.
summary: This paper provides a theoretical framework to identify the Griffiths phase in 3D antiferromagnetic and ferrimagnetic systems. It reveals that these systems exhibit much more complex magnetic signatures, such as upward deviations in inverse susceptibility, compared to standard ferromagnetic models. This work offers a way to experimentally distinguish true Griffiths singularities in disordered magnetic materials.
main_problem: The study aims to extend the theoretical understanding of the Griffiths phase (GP) from well-documented ferromagnetic systems to more complex antiferromagnetic and ferrimagnetic systems.
main_result: The researchers found that while ferromagnetic systems show a downward deviation in inverse susceptibility, antiferromagnetic systems exhibit an upward deviation, and ferrimagnetic systems show a complex crossover behavior including a compensation point where the system becomes analytic.
method: The study uses a Ginzburg-Landau-Wilson (GLW) framework incorporating quenched disorder, random dilution, and optimal fluctuation theory, supplemented by a quantum description of cluster magnetization.
model_or_system: The model consists of 3D spin-1/2 Ising ferromagnetic, antiferromagnetic, and ferrimagnetic systems subject to random dilution of magnetic sites.
key_observables: Magnetization (M), inverse susceptibility (chi^-1), differential susceptibility (chi_d), and the power-law exponent (lambda_H).
important_parameters: Disorder strength (p), temperature (T), magnetic field (H), and the percolation limit (pc = 0.6884).
main_assumptions: The study assumes a maximum cluster size of 7000 for computational tractability and uses a linear approximation for Tc suppression.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the effect of random dilution; Figure 2 displays inverse susceptibility and the temperature dependence of the exponent for FM systems; Figure 3 shows inverse susceptibility and magnetization for AFM systems; Figure 5 illustrates the power-law behavior and the compensation point in FiM systems.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of Griffiths phase identification, develops a theoretical framework using GLW theory, applies this framework to FM, AFM, and FiM systems, and concludes with a discussion on the utility of the proposed identification methods.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

A magnetic field of H = 0.5 Tesla was used for these calculations. In order to compare the χ−1 values calculated at different disor- der strength, p, the calculated χ−1 values were multiplied by a constant factor di for better comparison. The inset of (a) shows the variation of λg(T) as a function of disorder strength p; see the manuscript for details.
